data.title: Scorpio Tankers - Looking Closer At 2026E Oil Transportation
data.description:
Over the past few months, I have increased my analysis and coverage of the shipping sector. I've looked at TORM, and I've looked at Scandinavian shipping companies. Now the time has come to take a look at one of the more prominent and perhaps more generally well-known shipping companies, Scorpio Tankers (STNG). Unlike the others I've covered (at least some of them), this company comes with an NYSE ticker despite its Monaco base.

Much like its peers, the company operates a relatively modern fleet of tankers, and the focus, as with Frontline, is the transportation of various forms of fuel, oils, and other refined products. The company is relatively young, with a founding back in '09. What differs Scorpio from most of the companies I've looked at is that, unlike the companies covered previously, Scorpio is well along in the process of having eco-vessels. By fitting all of their vessels with significant emission scrubbing, the company has reached a point where the vast majority of its fleet can be considered eco-friendly - about 85% or so.

When comparing Scorpio to its peers, it's in no way wrong to say that the company has one of the "youngest" transportation fleets on the entire market, which obviously comes with both marketing and financial advantages. Why financial?

Aside from the obvious advantage of modern vessels using less fuel and maintenance, the adoption of green technology makes it possible for the companies in question to avoid rising carbon taxes - something the EU is particularly heavy on levying through the EU ETS—as well as avoid penalties for not complying with the sulphur tax, as some older vessels seem to want to do. The comparative asset value of lower vessels in the light of rising regulation will likely be declining more and more, and therefore, Scorpio is at heart an attractive company to look at.

Let's look at what else we have here.

Scorpio Tankers - Attractive and Perhaps Less Cyclical Than Most of Its Peers

One thing when I started looking at Scorpio was that I noted the company's share price history implies much less volatility than other shipping companies of the same type. It piqued my interest, so I set out to find out why this was the case.

As with all analyses, I started by looking at the filings and financials. I quickly found some signs and clarity as to why this was the case. Since about 4 years ago, the company has used whatever cash it has gotten to pay down significant debt, well over $2B, which has fundamentally transformed the typical shippers' balance sheet. We now have a balance sheet that is closing on net cash - and for a shipper, which typically operates a high-leverage, high-volatility, high-CapEx/OpEx sheet, this is materially different and improved. This is one.

But it enables me, as a valuation analyst and fundamentally oriented investor, to speak to you with clarity about the advantages of operating exactly this sort of balance sheet.

Let's say that Scorpio Tankers was to operate with net cash or very low debt (which the company, in some ways, already, at the time of writing, is doing). Obviously, debt reduction means less interest costs. This is one of the line items that typically eats up a lot of the cash flow. But more importantly, this impact means that the company's break-even has gone lower than many of its peers. I have written about Frontline, which had an operating breakeven in the low $20,000s for one ship. This was considered excellent. But would you be surprised if I told you that at some periods, STNG's break-even is as low as $12,500, or, according to management, as low as $11,000?

The modern fleet helps with this, for sure. But it's the dual power of asking investors (who are usually used to very high yields from shipping companies) to wait for returns while they address the debt situation. The company's dividend is one of the lower ones in the industry. At the time of writing, it stands at sub-risk-free rates, at a level of 2.38%.

That the share price has not been volatile does, of course, not mean that earnings history has not been volatile. It has. Nothing can take away from the volatility of the sector, and nothing has.

The trends in OI carry over, for the most part, into most other GAAP-related KPIs that I tend to use for analyzing. But perhaps more important is the decline in interest expense that the company has seen since 2021-2022, which is now at levels not seen in over 10 years.

Due to Scorpio leasing its vessels only to some of the highest-quality oil majors, the company has very low counterparty risk. It calls it a "blue-chip" customer base, and given what the customer base consists of, I would go ahead and agree firmly with this.

The company, technically speaking, has a higher fleet average age than Frontline, though not by much. And the core of the thesis is this: due to its reduction in debt, it has created a position where, in the case of relatively contextual small increases in pricing for its vessels, it can generate significant upside. As it stands, every increase of $10,000 per day generates almost $350M in annualized cash flow.

As with Frontline, the arguments for the company consist of and include closures of older refineries in previous legacy areas, which are reshaping the way that crude flows across the seas. It increases shipping activity across the globe, and given the current geopolitical situation, I don't see a whole lot of reason why this would change. It's also fair to say, using the company's own material as a source, that the company is leading in modern product tankers.

I believe history and pricing for newbuilding have shown that Scorpio Tankers has positioned itself very attractively for the coming market trends, with those coming market trends being high prices for shipping, high prices for newbuilds, and high prices for the services associated with them, as older vessels will have to be scrapped or used differently.

Bears on shipping companies can argue ESG and electrification as they wish, but there is no coming away from the fact that electrification is, in fact, still, and likely to remain, tied to the legacy market. Demand for seaborne product transport is likely to increase, or even in the very bearish scenario, stay stable. This is not only supported by refinery closures across APAC, Europe, and NA, but also by geopolitics. The most obvious current example is Iran - though for Scorpio, new exports from Venezuela are also a part of it.

Scorpio Tankers is therefore not only well-capitalized, but it is also what I would consider to be extremely well-capitalized and prepared. With cash/equivalents of close to a billion dollars in addition to revolvers, the company has about $1.7B of credit and cash on hand to cover any newbuilding commitments until beyond 2028, none of which are likely to even come close to straining this. The company is, in fact, currently net cash on the order of over $300M.

This means that among its peers, Scorpio Tankers is probably the company that is best prepared to take advantage of the operating leverage its situation and the market situation provide. It is categorically and logically better positioned than any peer with debt unless that peer has a similar fleet or a similar business model (which most do not).

4Q25, the latest report, already confirms some of this. Earnings are up, and the company also increased its dividend (although again, I consider the dividend probably the company's weak point on a comparative basis). We'll have to see how long sanctions and other issues close the market not only for Russian crude but for similar products as well. The fact that the "Russian shadow fleet" is a target for detention and action was confirmed less than 2 weeks ago, when Sweden seized the captain of a Russian vessel under suspicion of breaking the law - the ship is not, and is unlikely to be, released anytime soon.

I consider this likely to continue, with Great Britain having already confirmed the same.

In short, I believe Scorpio Tankers to be in a good position in terms of macro. I believe this position is backed by one of the, if not the, strongest balance sheets in the sector. This is an attractive position to be in. What's more, I give management an "A" rating in capital allocation and management because, from the time the company became higher-priced, management has not been buying back any significant amount of shares.

What you see above is a trend I consider to be one of the expert capital allocators. There are times when buybacks make sense - such as when you're buying stakes in company assets at below their very obvious market prices. But too often as of late, I have looked at companies that I consider executing share buybacks for the "wrong reasons".

This, dear readers, is not one of those companies.

Let me move on to valuation

Valuation for Scorpio Tankers

So, let me take a moment to step away from the positivity I have just been describing to clearly state that Scorpio is tied to the spot market for commodities. Therefore, a crash in spot market TCE rates or any of the correlated markets will and should impact this company's valuation and income. It has, and the company's fundamental improvements won't change that.

The typical valuation model for a shipping company takes its NAV. This is one of the approaches I also use with my peers. I will use the same logic I use with Frontline, which argues that in a bear market, asset value multiples can and should drop to somewhere between 0.55x and 0.8x or so. In a bullish market, as we are now, the NAV multiple will, consequently, rise to above 1x, and there will likely be a premium to the company. To get an upside, you can therefore either buy the company in a bear market at lows or in a bullish market below premium - the former is safer but unlikely to be possible for the near-term future. Unlike Frontline, the company's share price has not been close to NAV yet, and the current price/NAV per share considered by most analysts is still below 0.85x. However, it should be said that record lows can sometimes go as low as 0.4x here.

I would therefore, as a starting point, categorize 0.83x as a price where it's "high", but not necessarily representative of the current geopolitical trends. Secondly, I will use an EBITDA multiple. EBITDA is a good approach because the company's assets, the vessels, are subject to heavy D&A on the books, which makes P/E ratios a non-starter for shipping companies. We want to use EBITDA to see what cash is flowing and where. Shipping companies tend to be between 4x and 9.5x EBITDA, with Scorpio averaging between 5x and 8x (at extremes, it's been at 22x, but I shall ignore this as an outlier). I will use an NTM estimate instead of an LTM due to the current geopolitical situation. This puts the current EBITDA multiple at 6.88x, relative to a 10-year mean of 8.5x, but this includes the surge, so make it 7.8x. This implies to me, both from a NAV multiple and from an EBITDA multiple, that there might be room for the company to move up.

This is especially true given the current state of geopolitics.

Analysts tend to agree with my target ranges. I would argue a low of 6x EBITDA and a high of 8x, which, based on conservative assumptions, would put a floor of $66 and a high of about $87/share. The analyst averages go from $70/share to $90/share as a high, with a current average PT of $81/share. Out of 8 analysts, more than 75% of them are considering the company a "BUY".

I will state that $75/share may be undervaluing this company for a number of reasons. First off, NAV. Most analysts put the NAV at the very least at $85/share. I agree with that assessment. In this environment, buying a ship (which is what you are doing if you buy shares) at a 15-20% discount is, by definition relative to the market, a good valuation deal.

And I am all about good valuation deals.

Furthermore, this share price underestimates the impact of surges in TCE. While we don't know how long the surges in TCE rates will last, it's a material and objective fact that the companies in the sector will see cash flow during 2026 from these surges. Scorpio is set to earn a lot more. This needs to be considered, and I do not see that being the case here.

Third, the net debt (or lack of it). I don't see that the company is being premiumized for its net cash, which it, in my view, should be.

As a result of this, I will give the company a PT of $76.5/share, which fulfills my requirement of 15% annualized or above to a fair value, inclusive of the dividend. Note that the company's fair value is different from the price target and instead represents the price at which I would consider the company fully valued (and potentially a rotation target).

At $76.5, the upside of 15% or above per year is still slim, but it is there. I may be considered more conservative than some (or most), but this is once again because of my "bar" in terms of returns. The fact that the company's yield is still low impacts this assumption quite heavily.

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a $76.5/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized, which the company is now doing, based on an upside to the company's fair value (not PT). I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "BUY" rating.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a price target of $76.5/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of March 2026.

data.title: Meta: A Deeper Look At Its Capex Burdens
data.description:
Performance assessment

I was very wrong on my bullish view on Meta (META):

Elevator pitch

I am doing a 180 degree turn on my stance on Meta:

  1. Child safety regulations and addiction lawsuits can threaten user engagement and growth metrics
  2. Funding of own power generation infrastructure can rack up capex costs
  3. Market flows are shifting away from the Mag7 and social media companies
  4. META stock is valued at an absolute and relative discount
  5. The charts show a bearish rollover

Child safety regulations and addiction lawsuits can threaten user engagement and growth metrics

The world is increasingly moving to ban social media for young adults. In my recent Reddit (RDDT) article, I had shared the following:

Social media is getting more addictive. And there is an ever-growing body of research warning about the detrimental impacts of that addiction on mental health and productivity. Last year, some governments, such as Denmark, started to plan for bans on social media access for youth. My country of Australia has led the way in becoming the first nation to ban social media for teens in late Dec'25. And other countries such as New Zealand, Malaysia, the UK and more are expected to follow as curbing social media access to youth is becoming a worldwide trend.

- Hunting Alphas' article on Reddit

That said, I will mention that the broader trend of global social media bans has only gained further traction as the Czech Republic and Indonesia have also joined the wagon to outright ban social media for people aged under 15 and 16, respectively.

In addition to the social media ban headwinds, Meta is also facing a barrage of lawsuits. For example, it was fined $365M for violating child safety laws and $4.2M for inducing social media addiction:

One ruling was that the company's apps failed to protect children from sexual exploitation, for which it was fined $375M, and the other held it liable for inducing social media addiction in a young woman and was penalized about $4.2M.

However, the latter ruling by the LA jury has stirred a serious buzz, with many calling it a “Big Tobacco” moment, as the case is being labeled a bellwether for pending and upcoming lawsuits to hold social media platforms accountable for the addictive design of their products.

- Seeking Alpha News

Now, these penalty amounts are peanuts to Meta, making up only 0.63% of the company's FY25 PAT in total. However, the bigger risk impact is that the success of these lawsuits makes Meta vulnerable to a lot more similar lawsuits in future. And even more importantly, I expect it to accelerate the trends of anti-social media controls, particularly on young adults. And I posit that this will hurt social media companies like Meta via the following mechanism:

Many people get habituated to using Facebook and Instagram, and for that matter, other social media platforms, during their school and college days, when they have more friends and free time. So curbing access to social media during these young ages reduces the window of opportunity for people to become loyal, high-frequency social media users or addicts.

At the end of the day, if my thesis here is right, we should expect DAPs to see a slower rate of growth, or even a decline:

Funding of own power generation infrastructure can rack up capex costs

US policy direction is moving toward requiring major tech companies such as Meta that run large data centers to pay for their own electricity going forward for new data centers:

Tonight, I'm pleased to announce that I have negotiated the new ratepayer protection pledge. You know what that is? We're telling the major tech companies that they have the obligation to provide for their own power needs... We have an old grid. It could never handle the kind of numbers, the amount of electricity that's needed. So I'm telling them, they can build their own plant. They're going to produce their own electricity.

- US President Donald Trump

Recently, Meta went into an agreement with Entergy (ETR), wherein Meta will be footing the bill for:

  • Seven new natural gas-fueled combined-cycle power plants totaling more than 5,200 megawatts, with capability for future carbon capture and hydrogen co-firing
  • Approximately 240 miles of new 500 kV transmission lines connecting South Louisiana to North Louisiana and Arkansas
  • Battery energy storage across three locations
  • Nuclear power uprates
  • Commitment from Meta to help fund up to 2,500 megawatts of new renewable resources
  • Memorandum of understanding to explore the future development and use of nuclear power

- Entergy Press Release, HA's bolded highlights

I expect this to impact Meta's $10B 1GW Indiana data center as well.

So the critical question is what would the financial impact of all this be? Here's how I'm working through it:

First, I computed the cost rates for power and transmission line infrastructure using known information from a similar Meta - Entergy deal last year:

Then, based on this power infrastructure cost rate of 1.41 USD/W and transmission line cost rate of 2,400 USD/V, I computed the total infrastructure cost of the latest Entergy deal to be $8.6B:

But I still compute total power and transmission cost rates based on data center values and data center capacities for this project. This gives me a crude average for power and transmission infrastructure for data centers:

This lets me estimate the power and transmission infrastructure cost of the Indiana data center to be $2.8B:

So adding up the Entergy deal's infrastructure cost and the expected costs of the Indiana data center up, we get a total incremental cost of $11.3B:

Ultimately, from a capex impact perspective, this represents a 9.1% total cost hike, counting only these major data center projects:

Now, the chart above presents the data as if all of this infrastructure cost will be borne in FY26. Of course, some of it may spill over to FY27 too. But the broader point is that due to this new policy, pledges, capex costs can be hiked by almost 10%. And this is a clear headwind for Meta because these cost increases are not offset by higher revenues.

Market flows are shifting away from the Mag7 and social media companies

META is a core stock in the Magnificent 7 group, as can be seen in the Roundhill's Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS):

The Magnificent 7 stocks make up a large chunk of the overall S&P500 market capitalization share:

And this has contributed to overall S&P500 (SPY) outperformance vs the equal-weighted S&P500 (RSP):

But so far this YTD, the Mag7 stocks are correcting hard. And Meta has performed the 2nd-worst YTD after Microsoft (MSFT):

I believe this is due to greater headwinds hitting social media companies, something I discussed in more depth in my last piece on Reddit. Indeed, the industry rotation graphs support this narrative, as the Social Media industry factor has been the worst performing:

Overall, this is part of a broader theme wherein money flows are leaving technology and AI stocks in favor of heavy assets plays and consumer staples. Hence, from this market sentiment analysis, the verdict for META stock is not very encouraging.

META stock is valued at an absolute and relative discount

META is trading at a 1-yr FWD P/E of 17.4x, which is a moderate 33% discount to its longer-term median multiple of 25.9x.

Relative to social media company peers, META's 1-yr FWD P/E is 10% higher than the comps' median of 15.8x:

But this does not mean overvaluation. In fact, it suggests the opposite because Meta has typically commanded a much higher premium vs its peers of 22.8%:

So the lower 10% premium now indicates relative undervaluation.

The charts show a bearish rollover

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

META Technical Analysis

META stock is displaying strong bearish momentum due to 2 bearish engulfs after a rejection from monthly resistance. And there is still some downside left until the next major monthly support level is hit.

Takeaway & Positioning

As a recap, this shows the breakup of my view on X stock based on different drivers, as well as an overall assessment:

  • Fundamental Thesis: Bearish due to headwinds from social media bans, more potential addiction lawsuits, and an almost 10% capex cost hike
  • Sentiment Analysis: Bearish due to rotation out of Mag7 and especially social media stocks
  • Valuation: Bullish due to discounted valuations
  • Technical Analysis: Bearish due to strong seller momentum
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Bearish, 'Sell.'

data.title: BlackRock Low Duration Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
• The fund posted returns of 1.28% (Institutional shares) and 1.22% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the fourth quarter of 2025.

• The fund's outperformance of its benchmark was led by agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS), investment grade and high yield credit, duration (interest rate sensitivity) and yield curve positioning, commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), and asset-backed securities (ABS).

• The fund's long duration position was increased, which reflected an economic backdrop that was supportive of potential Federal Reserve (Fed) monetary policy easing and a path of least resistance toward lower yields. Spread assets tightened and the fund selectively trimmed exposure in areas of richness.

Contributors

Overweight exposures to both agency MBS and investment grade credit were the largest contributors to performance. The low volatility caused by a decrease in Fed policy uncertainty supported MBS valuation significantly, with favorable supply and demand a tailwind for corporate credit, while years of above-trend growth have strengthened corporates' financial positions. As such, demand from all-in yield buyers has been robust, which has allowed spreads to compress. Similar themes apply to ABS and CMBS, which performed positively overall.

Detractors

An underweight exposure to U.S. Treasuries marginally detracted, as the sector did well during the quarter.

data.title: Micron: Buy This Selloff Driven By TurboQuant And Sora
data.description:
Introduction

In my previous article on Micron Technology (MU), I laid out the case for why the memory industry has structurally changed. The combination of slowing DRAM scaling, rising capital intensity, and strong AI-driven demand led me to believe that the traditional boom-bust cycle in memory is unlikely to repeat, making Micron a compelling investment.

Since then, Micron has reported a blowout quarter, but the stock has pulled back anyway. In this update, I want to break down what drove the decline, and more importantly, why I believe the current concerns make this an attractive buying opportunity.

Earnings

On March 18, Micron delivered an FQ2 topline beat of 22.3% and a bottom line beat of 39.7%. Even more impressive, Micron delivered a top-line guidance beat of 44% and a bottom-line guidance beat of 77.8%.

The sentiment afterward was euphoric! For a company with a market cap nearing half a trillion to deliver this kind of beat screamed of another NVIDIA (NVDA) moment. However, the immediate reaction was a steep decline, which left many investors scratching their heads.

I believe the reason for the reaction was the increased CAPEX guide to $25B from $20B. Historically speaking, the time to buy a memory stock has been after a lot of capacity has left the industry and there's a catalyst that will trigger an increase in demand. Conversely, the ideal time to sell would be when there are lots of new announcements for new plants, not when the earnings turn down. The reason for this is because expansion plans typically meant that future earnings would go down, and the stock market is forward-looking. Seasoned memory cycle investors follow this playbook religiously; it doesn't matter if "this time is different".

However, there is good reason to believe this time is actually different, and seasoned memory cycle investors may be the ones that look like fools this time around, exiting too early. I won't go into depth into the structural reasons that memory is no longer cyclical since I already covered them in my first Micron article here, but what I would like to add is the confirmation of the industry's first 5-year SCA confirmed by Micron in the latest earnings call. The details of the SCA are not publicly released, but Micron's CEO Sanjay Mehrotra stated:

What I want to highlight is that these SCAs are multiyear and they have specific commitments in them. These are robust agreements, and these are meant to give us the visibility and stability toward out business model."

I believe that Sanjay's words are confirmation that the increased CAPEX guide is backed by long-term visibility, and the 5-year SCA enforces my view. If I am correct on one of the selloff drivers being the increased CAPEX guide, I believe this is an attractive buying opportunity.

TurboQuant

On March 24, Google Research introduced TurboQuant. They claimed their new compression algorithm reduces LLM key-value cache memory by at least 6 times while delivering 8 times more speed and 0 accuracy loss.

This new algorithm made headlines, added downside pressure across memory stocks, and raised some eyebrows on the future of memory demand. However, after further digging, a couple of key facts were revealed. Firstly, it was discovered that the TurboQuant paper was first submitted on April 28, 2025, so it obviously did not prevent the memory supercycle from continuing, and it makes little sense for memory stocks to sell off on something nearly a year old. Second, the TurboQuant paper contains serious issues in how it describes RaBitQ, including incorrect technical claims, misleading theory, and misleading experiment comparisons. These concerns were flagged by the RaBitQ authors before the submission of TurboQuant, and they were acknowledged but not fixed. With that being said, the huge promotion and fear created by TurboQuant seem to be very overblown and not a genuine concern. In my opinion, the TurboQuant fears should be bought.

Sora

On March 24, OpenAI announced they are shutting down Sora. This was the result of high compute costs, minimal monetization, and a strategic shift toward more focused initiatives. While this was not talked about much in regard to the memory stock sell-off, I believe this was a major driver since Sora was a prime example of highly memory-intensive AI workloads. Aside from the actual demand destruction caused by the shutdown, Sora's shutdown also raises concerns that these video generation workloads may be less economically viable than previously expected, given the massive infrastructure and memory requirements they impose. In that context, memory stocks selling off makes sense since one of the leading AI players is stepping away, leaving investors to question how sustainable the current memory demand really is.

However, I think this reaction is not justified since this likely misrepresents the true meaning behind Sora's shutdown. The issue is not a lack of demand for memory-intensive AI workloads, but rather the current cost of delivering them at scale. When costs are too high, companies are forced to optimize and improve efficiency. This does not eliminate demand; it just delays and reshapes it. If anything, the fact that Sora was shut down shows how much demand there truly is and how resource-heavy those workloads truly are. Sora's shutdown will leave video generation demand building beneath the surface, waiting for the next stage of optimization and deployment to unlock it once again. As efficiency improves and infrastructure scales, I'm confident more platforms similar to Sora will return in a more economically viable and sustainable form.

Risks

While I view Micron's increased CAPEX and 5-year SCA as a sign of long-term visibility, the market is clearly still seeing it through the traditional boom-and-bust cycle lens. If all three major players, Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix, ramp up capacity too aggressively, it could recreate the supply dynamics seen in previous cycles. However, I believe this risk is insignificant due to the structural trends in the memory industry that I covered in my first Micron article here.

TurboQuant, while flawed, also highlights a potential future risk. As the industry tries to reduce memory requirements through efficiency gains and compression, the rapidly increasing demand could be challenged. As of now, there is nothing to indicate these efficiency gains are disrupting demand, but I will be sure to monitor any future developments and update readers when a notable shift occurs.

Conclusion

I believe this recent selloff is being driven more by short-term fear around CAPEX, TurboQuant, and Sora's shutdown than any real deterioration in Micron's long-term demand outlook. While these developments have understandably raised confusion and concerns around efficiency gains reducing memory demand and the viability of video generation workloads, I view them as noise within a much greater structural shift in the memory industry. When viewed alongside multi-year SCA agreements highlighting demand visibility, I see this pullback as a buying opportunity rather than a warning sign.

Thus, I would like to reiterate my rating of MU as a "Strong Buy."

data.title: MDYG: A Solid Mid-Cap ETF To Ride Recovery And Earn Good Return Over Long Term
data.description:
State Street SPDR S&P 400 Mid Cap Growth ETF's (MDYG) war-driven share price drop of 8.5% makes it an attractive investment vehicle for dip buyers and long-term investors. The mid-cap growth ETF trades at cheap valuations with a robust earnings growth power compared to the large-cap growth category. MDYG's price momentum and expense ratio are better than other popular mid-cap growth funds. Moreover, data shows that the sweet spot of the market has the potential to beat large caps during a recovery phase. Therefore, I initiate coverage of MDYG with a buy rating.

Fear Presents a Buying Opportunity

An extreme fear over the Middle East war triggered a broader selloff in the US stock market, wiping out 8% of the S&P 500 value from its 52-week high. Whereas, the NASDAQ and mega-caps fell into the correction territories, as their prices plunged more than 10% from the latest 52-week high.

The mid-cap category, which significantly outperformed large caps in the past six months, has also been responding to the broader stock market volatility. The S&P MidCap 400 Index is down more than 6% since the war broke out in the Middle East. The mid-cap growth ETFs, including MDYG, declined faster than the broader mid-cap index.

Legendary investors, including Warren Buffett, use the market volatility as a buying opportunity. I believe it is the time to implement Warren Buffett's strategy of buying when others fear. History tells us that wars can create a temporary decline, with markets sharply recovering losses and soaring to new highs in the short to mid-term. In addition, the market is likely to bottom out shortly, as the current selloff has already exceeded an average decline of 6% during wars.

Mid-Caps Seem More Attractive Than Large-Caps

Prior to the war, the mid-cap category significantly outperformed large caps as the spree of rate cuts accelerated investors rotation from expensive large caps to cheap mid-caps. Consequently, despite the war-driven selloff, the S&P MidCap 400 Index is trading in green year-to-date compared to the S&P 500's negative price return of more than 5%.

Currently, the mid-cap category seems to be an attractive place for dip buyers because of its cheap valuations and strong earnings growth power.

For example, the S&P MidCap 400 Index trades below 16x forward earnings compared to the S&P 500's 19.7x. In addition, the S&P MidCap 400 Index is poised to experience nearly 20% earnings per share growth in 2026, beating the S&P 500's forecasted growth of 15%.

In addition, the mid-cap category has the potential to beat the large caps during a recovery phase due to its lower exposure to international markets and higher revenue concentration in domestic markets. Furthermore, the S&P MidCap 400 Index appears appealing due to its well-balanced portfolio with better stock diversification. The industrials sector makes up 25% of index weight, while its tilt towards the technology sector is around 20%.

Why Is MDYG a Solid Candidate?

As the market is expected to enter a recovery phase in the weeks ahead, selecting the high-beta, mid-cap, growth-focused ETFs, such as State Street SPDR S&P 400 Mid Cap Growth ETF, could enable investors to make heftier gains than the broader market. This is because the high-beta ETFs have the potential to soar sharply during a recovery phase and over the long term.

Prior to the war, MDYG demonstrated a strong momentum. I believe the ETF is likely to regain its momentum once the war-driven volatility fades. In addition, MDYG's uptrend is expected to be supported by the strong fundamentals of its key sectors.

The industrials sector accounts for 30% of MDYG's entire portfolio weight, while the technology sector makes up 20%. Year to date, the S&P MidCap 400 Industrials sector is up 5%. MDYG's top industrials stocks, such as Curtiss-Wright Corporation (CW), XPO, Inc. (XPO), and Woodward, Inc. (WWD), continue to deliver blistering price growth.

On the other hand, unlike a double-digit decline from the large-cap tech sector, the mid-cap tech stocks have been thriving due to their robust growth potential and cheap valuations. Year to date, the S&P MidCap 400 Information Technology sector is up nearly 9%. MDYG's top tech stocks are Lumentum Holdings (LITE), Coherent Corp. (COHR), and Flex Ltd. (FLEX). Year to date, the share price of Lumentum is up 100%, while Coherent's and Flex's shares soared 33% and 6%, respectively.

Other key sectors in MDYG's portfolio are health care, consumer cyclical, financials, real estate, and consumer defensive. The energy, materials, and real estate sectors are benefiting from cyclical tailwinds.

MDYG trades around 20x its forward twelve-month earnings, down from the large-cap growth PE ratio of 24x. Its price-to-book and price-to-sales ratios are better than the large-cap category. The fund's other key characteristics include a low expense ratio, above-average liquidity, and strong price momentum in its peer group.

iShares Morningstar Mid-Cap Growth ETF (IMCG), iShares Russell Mid-Cap Growth ETF (IWP), and Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF (VOT) are among the popular options in the mid-cap growth category.

These ETFs have underperformed compared to MDYG because of their exposure to an upper end of the mid-cap market. On the other hand, MDYG offers exposure to the core mid-cap growth category with a median portfolio market cap of $13 billion. The fund's expense ratio of 0.15% appears low, while liquidity seems above average with a three-month average shares trading volume of 88K. MDYG is ranked at the 6th spot in its sub-asset class, according to Seeking Alpha quant analysis.

Risk Factors to Consider

There are numerous risk factors to consider when investing in mid-caps. The category is sensitive to the broader market trends and interest rates. In the case of a recession or surprise shift in interest rate policy, the mid-caps can come under pressure. In addition, the large-cap-driven bull run can also reduce investor confidence in mid-caps. MDYG's standard deviation and annualized volatility of 11.7% and 16% are higher than the median of all ETFs.

In Conclusion

The latest broader market selloff presents a compelling buying opportunity for investors, as the war-driven selloffs are often temporary. The mid-cap category appears well-positioned to resume its uptrend once volatility fades. In particular, the high-beta investment vehicles, such as State Street SPDR S&P 400 Mid Cap Growth ETF, have the potential to generate market-beating returns. In addition, its expense ratio is low, liquidity is above average, and quant score is higher than peers. MDYG also appears more attractive than the large-cap growth category due to its cheap valuations and robust earnings growth power.

data.title: Deep Yellow Limited Inches Closer To Production
data.description:
Deep Yellow Limited (DYLLF) is nearing the early stages of ramping up production, with its flagship Tumas project nearing FID. With early development underway, the project may commence production within 3 years of FID, delivering first shipments in 2029 at the earliest. With the global energy economy potentially shifting gears following the emerging events in the Middle East, I suspect energy security will become top priority, potentially favoring nuclear power in tandem with natural gas. With the market in the early stages of growth, I believe DYLLF can secure an appealing position as a critical uranium supplier in the coming years; I am recommending shares with a Buy rating with a price target of $1.43/share, assuming $80/lb uranium.

DYLLF Operational Update

DYLLF is making progress towards bringing its Tumas project to life, preparing to declare FID on the Tumas project to begin construction and mine development. Accordingly, management sees that uranium prices are sufficient to bring the greenfield start-up into the development and production phases. As of March 2026, the project is fully permitted with power secured for operations.

In total, DYLLF has 430Mlbs of resources across its 4 major projects, with an estimated 30-year mine life for the Tumas project. Tumas and Mulga are the first two projects DYLLF has planned for development once uranium prices support FID. According to its March 2026 corporate update, Tumas is progressing with Phase 3 detailed engineering, 65% complete, with 70% of equipment tendered. Once FID is reached, Tumas can begin development, which is expected to cost $474mm in an initial capital outlay to source infrastructure and construction resources to ready for mine development. Once fully ramped up, DYLLF is expected to produce 3.6Mlbs per year with an all-in sustaining cost of $45/lb of U₃O₈; U₃O₈, triuranium octoxide, is a primary form of yellowcake uranium concentrate. It is the most stable form of uranium oxide, used to store and transport uranium between mines and conversion facilities.

The Mulga Rock project is also progressing after being granted the appropriate mining leases. The project has an estimated 104.8Mlbs of U₃O₈ and other critical minerals like cobalt, copper, and nickel. Mulga Rock is currently undergoing test work to determine the value of the project, potentially years away from FID.

Looking at the big picture, Tumas should be nearing FID, providing an outline for first mineral production. Taking into consideration DYLLF’s timeline, we should expect first production in 3 years following FID, placing the project online in 2029 at the earliest.

DYLLF had a major change of leadership at the start of 2026 following CEO John Borshoff’s departure in October 2025. In February 2026, DYLLF announced the appointment of Managing Director & CEO Greg Field. Field was previously Managing Director of Projects for Rio Tinto, overseeing the Americas and Europe, and has a deep history working in the industry.

Macroeconomic Outlook

From a macroeconomic perspective, first production may align with new developments in the works as reactors are either being newly constructed or brought back online. In October 2025, the US government signed a partnership with Westinghouse Electric to build $80b worth of AP1000 nuclear reactors and small modular reactors [SMRs] in the US. AP1000 reactors have 1,110MWe in net electrical output.

Accordingly, the Trump administration called for 10 reactors to be under construction by 2030, an ambitious goal given the current energy landscape. According to Reuters, the most recent two Westinghouse reactors built in the US cost $35b, more than double the initial estimate for the projects and 7 years behind schedule.

In addition to this, two BWRX-300 SMRs are being developed by GE Vernova (GEV), each with 300MW of capacity.

In the near future, 15 reactors are expected to come online in 2026, adding roughly 12GW of new capacity to global nuclear power production. More than 50 reactors are expected to commence operations from 2027 to 2030. According to the World Nuclear Association, there are currently 75 reactors under construction and an additional 120 reactors planned, many of which will be located in China. Reactors presently under construction will add an estimated 80.6GWe, with 110GWe planned. Further, there is roughly 300GWe of capacity that has been proposed with uncertainty as to start time.

Uranium prices have been somewhat volatile over recent years after spot prices climbed to north of $100/lb in early 2024 before moderating down to the mid-$60-80/lb range. While the economic outlook remains robust for uranium, I suspect the near-term interest in natural gas electricity generation may overshadow the prospects for the total addressable market for nuclear power. While planned and proposed reactors create an optimistic picture for the future of the industry, I believe the energy sector will be assessing the balance between the feasibility of not only adding infrastructure but also sourcing adequate quantities of uranium in order to power these reactors. If uranium cannot be secured, I cannot see a reason for such high demand for nuclear reactor development.

That being said, recent events in the Middle East may be one of the biggest hidden demand drivers for the transition from natural gas to uranium over time. Accordingly, roughly 20% of the world’s supply of oil and natural gas has been taken offline following missile strikes on critical gas export infrastructure in the Middle East, complicating global trade for LNG. With international natural gas benchmarks reflecting a tighter gas market, I can see a number of outcomes over the coming years to derisk the global energy trade.

  1. Increased development of US LNG exports, creating more global dependency on US gas.
  2. Accelerated transition to alternative energy resources like nuclear and renewables.

What we know is that natural gas is abundantly available in the US and is one of the lowest-costing fuels for power production. Using GE Vernova as a core example, the company currently has slot reservations for 83GW of natural gas turbine capacity. US LNG export capacity is expected to grow by 20Bcf/d+ through 2035, up from 15.4Bcf/d in 2025. I believe the global energy supply will be determined by two factors:

  1. Can enriched uranium supply be secured?
  2. Can US gas production augment global demand?

While both of these factors can progress in tandem, I believe natural gas may be more heavily favored given the availability of resources and the lack of need to store spent fuel.

Risks Related to Deep Yellow Limited

Bull Case

DYLLF is nearing FID for the Tumas project, a major development in the company’s multi-year investment story. With the mine expected to be highly profitable, DYLLF should realize positive cash flows in the first year of production.

Bear Case

First production may be pushed back depending on the risks involved in the nuclear fuel cycle. With interest in natural gas picking up at the global scale, regions may pull back on nuclear development ambitions.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

DYLLF shares are currently trading at 29.27x EV/Resource Value of its known assets using the price assumption of $80/lb of uranium. Assuming first production begins in 2029, I believe DYLLF should be valued at $1.43/share at a 10% discount rate, assuming $80/lb uranium; I am recommending shares with a Buy rating. In terms of trading the stock, DYLLF's share price may react to new developments relating to the Tumas project as well as to the price changes of uranium.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Invesco SteelPath MLP Alpha Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 MLPs total return outperformed the broader equity market

For the quarter, master limited partnerships (MLPs), as measured by the Alerion MLP Index (AMZ), rose 1.81% on a price basis and returned 3.79% when including distributions. The S&P 500 Index rose 2.35% on a price basis and had a total return of 2.66%.

2 Better-than-expected operating results

During the quarter, 62% of midstream sector participants reported third quarter results in line with or better than consensus. Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation & amortization (EBITDA) were about 5% higher than the second quarter and about 7% above the third quarter of 2024.

3 We remain focused on the long-term investment horizon

We believe valuations remain attractive and fundamentals support expectations for cash flow growth. Further, the rise of artificial intelligence (AI) models has been increasing power demand from data centers, which may meaningfully increase demand for natural gas.

Manager perspective and outlook

• West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude oil priced at the Cushing hub ended the quarter at $57.42 per barrel, down 8% from the end of the third quarter and 20% lower than one year ago. The price spread between Brent crude, a proxy for international crude prices, and WTI ended at $3.43 per barrel, narrowing during the quarter. Crude oil priced in Midland, Texas maintained a premium relative to WTI as crude pipeline capacity out of the Permian basin remained sufficient and there appeared to be an incentive for incremental volumes to move toward the Gulf Coast and export markets.

• Henry Hub natural gas prices ended the quarter at $3.69 per million British thermal units (MMBtu), up 12% from the end of the third quarter and 1% higher than one year ago. Gas pricing in the Permian Basin ended the quarter at negative levels as production growth combined with pipeline maintenance activities reduced available transport from the basin.

• Natural gas liquids (NGLs) priced at Mont Belvieu ended the quarter at $24.76 per barrel, down 10% from last quarter and 25% lower than one year ago. Prices for all NGL purity products declined during the quarter; propane declined the least while isobutane had the largest price decline for the quarter. Frac spreads, a measure of natural gas processing economics, settled at $0.24 per gallon, down 37% from the end of the third quarter and 51% lower than one year ago.

Portfolio positioning

For the quarter, MLPs were up 1.81% on a price basis and returned 3.79% when including distributions. The S&P 500 Index was up 2.35% on a price basis and had a total return of 2.66%.

No US midstream equity (common or preferred) was issued during the quarter via marketed transactions. We estimate a de minimis amount of equity, if any, was issued through “at-the-market” programs. Many sector participants have continued to buy back stock as attractive valuations have in our view persisted. Buyback disclosures historically and typically accompany earnings reports which trail the quarter end by several weeks (for the third quarter of 2025, approximately $1.7 billion of buybacks were reported).

We estimate MLP-focused investment vehicles, including closed-end funds, open-end funds and index-linked products, had approximately $0.2 billion of net inflows during the quarter.

MLP capital investment included an estimated $7-8 billion of organic capital spending. Producer growth plans have remained moderate and have kept traditional midstream growth capital spending requirements measured. However, projects serving rising power demand have been adding incremental growth avenues while leaving sector participants with free cash available for debt retirement, unit repurchases and distribution increases in current and future periods.

During the quarter, the 10-year US Treasury Bond fell by 0.02% to end the period at 4.18%. The MLP yield spread at quarter end, relative to the 10-year Treasury, narrowed by 0.10% to 3.52%. The long-term average (2000-4Q2025) is 4.33%. At quarter end, the AMZ's yield was 7.69%.

We believe the midstream sector offers investors an attractive distribution yield and an improving outlook for cash flow and distribution growth. In our view, these fundamentals are supported by significant and predictable natural gas volume growth through the end of the decade, alongside stable to modest growth in crude oil volumes. Further, midstream sector leverage and distribution coverage metrics have in our view remained healthy, which we believe insulates sector growth plans from market volatility.

Performance highlights

The propane and diversified sub-sectors provided the best relative returns for the quarter, each benefiting from idiosyncratic factors affecting certain sub-sector members. The other and compression sub-sectors had the lowest average returns. Performance in the other group was weighed down by two companies focused on wholesale refined products; the compression sub-sector was likely affected by merger and acquisition activity.

Contributors to performance

Targa Resources Corp (TRGP)

TRGP outperformed after reporting better-than-expected financial and operating results and reaffirming company expectations for low double-digit volume growth in 2026. During the quarter, TRGP announced two strategically complementary acquisitions in the Permian Basin that are expected to boost earnings. TRGP provides midstream natural gas and natural gas liquid services via an integrated asset base with a premium footprint in the Permian basin.

MPLX LP (MPLX)

MPLX outperformed after reporting financial results that were in line with expectations and announcing a 12.5% increase in its quarterly cash distribution. MPLX has continued to advance its strategy of creating integrated crude oil and natural gas logistics systems from the Permian to the Gulf Coast.

Plains All American Pipeline LP (PAA)

PAA outperformed after acquiring the EPIC Crude Oil Pipeline, an 800-mile long-haul crude oil pipeline from the Permian and Eagle Ford basins to the Gulf Coast.

PAA has maintained a leading position in crude oil gathering and transportation from the Permian Basin, exhibits in our view strong distribution coverage and has an improved balance sheet.

Detractors from performance

Energy Transfer LP (ET)

ET underperformed after reporting earnings below expectations and reducing its 2025 EBITDA guidance. ET has announced several projects, including projects addressing rising natural gas demand, that will likely increase its leverage to the Permian Basin. ET has one of the largest and most diversified portfolios of midstream assets in the US.

Cheniere Energy Inc. (LNG)

LNG underperformed as price and volume outlooks for global natural gas appear to be moderating as new liquefaction facilities are expected to add supply at a faster pace than expected demand growth. LNG's contract portfolio historically largely insulates it from moderating global natural gas prices. LNG owns and operates liquefied natural gas (LNG) terminals and pipelines in Louisiana and Texas.

The Williams Companies Inc (WMB) (WMB)

WMB underperformed despite reporting financial and operating results generally in line with expectations. WMB advanced several growth project initiatives with its burgeoning power solutions business, as well as new pipeline projects within its core natural gas gathering and transmission segments.

data.title: ConocoPhillips: Oman Pleas Highlight Industry Uncertainty
data.description:
ConocoPhillips (COP) has suffered damages as a result of the Iranian situation. Oil industry executives in general are frustrated by the uncertainties caused mostly by indefinite communication about the Iranian situation. This is the latest in a long string of frustrations voiced by industry executives, including ConocoPhillips executives, about what the industry needs compared to what the industry is getting (or lack thereof).

" Conoco (COP), which owns a 30% stake in QatarEnergy's Ras Laffan LNG facility—the world's largest—that was closed after suffering substantial damage in an Iranian attack, is "pleading" with the Trump administration for military "protection around the U.S.-owned assets in Qatar and hundreds of millions of dollars of investment," Lance said."

This is coming from a Seeking Alpha article that was referenced in the second sentence of this article. It is far from the only thing that industry executives are concerned about. The industry itself was far from satisfied about the whole situation with the administration before the Iranian situation probably made things more challenging. I wrote about this all last year.

Now the administration wants more production from the industry to bring oil prices down. But things do not work that way.

Profit Requirements

In an industry that is notoriously volatile and uncertain, this industry requires some darn good communication about administration intentions along with good solid follow-up. But the execution side is very changeable (to say the least). Therefore, to expect the industry to increase production at a time when oil prices are uncertain and there is little to no guidance that would lead the industry to expect a certain range of oil prices is asking a bit much.

Note that this just came out because the questions were asked March 16-19. In the comments section of this same report, there is a general wait-and-see attitude because no one in any industry spends money unless they are certain that they can make a profit one way or another.

What is particularly telling here is that smaller firms, which represent about 20% of production, are far more likely to increase production than larger firms. But that is not going to make much of a difference when oil prices rise.

Furthermore, the industry pointed to pipeline constraints that take years of planning and construction as another challenge to increasing production to help make up for the current shortage.

For those that thought that Venezuela was going to be the solution, that is yet another situation where the industry wants evidence of some stability and support, or they likewise will not invest. Several in the industry mentioned balance sheet strengthening, which usually happens first in any price increase situation.

Even back in the "go-go" days of fairly rapid growth, at no time did the United States simply increase production by the millions in a matter of days or "on request". That huge lack of understanding of the industry has long been an industry complaint of this administration.

Last Article

The last article on ConocoPhillips noted that the Marathon (MRO) acquisition was basically a good thing for the company. Roughly one year later, the assumptions that made that move a good move are being "blown apart" by the uncertainty of the Iranian situation. United States assets probably will benefit from higher commodity prices. The question is the significance of those higher prices brought about by the uncertainty of the Iranian situation.

Unpredictability

Senior executives of the industry voiced specific concerns about the situation. Shown below is a small sample from the survey of the comments that came back from the special questions section:

What is notable about this is how the current situation is at times being related to the uncertainty of all the tariff strategy changes and other economic strategies that are critical to this industry. This is really a follow-up with a lot more detail to a frequent industry comment about the administration of "they do not understand, and they do not want to understand" combined with "they want what they want when they want it." There is a lot more detail, and actually even more detail than I can fit in an article.

The issue here is that industry activity did increase for the first time in three quarters. But as the only graph in the article concludes, that is not going to lead to production increases.

In an industry that is expected to be friendly to this administration, clearly something very different is happening.

Loss

While smaller firms can be expected to largely benefit from higher oil prices, the same is not true of larger (especially) international firms. As ConocoPhillips inadvertently pointed out, there are many large firms in the Middle East that have damage to their operations there from the Iranian missiles. This could very well lead to some large losses that could possibly exceed any immediate benefits of higher oil prices.

In fact, there could be a number of large firms that report a production decline due to those missile attacks on our allies. In short, world production could well come down some percentage even if the situation were resolved "tomorrow". It is going to take some time to get back to where things were before this crisis or challenge began.

Surprisingly, the situation that has brought about higher oil prices (which everyone is complaining about) may actually result in some brutal quarterly reports going forward. This accounts for some of the market's confusion about forward commodity prices as well as how to value the industry going forward.

Summary

ConocoPhillips was one of several companies that are none too happy about this situation. To me, this confirms a common comment I hear about the public not being sold on the Iranian situation before we began it. This may be the first time I believe that the "sale" has not occurred, and frankly it is liable to be the last the way things are unfolding.

But the biggest thing is that the changeability of administration strategies in a number of key areas is being seen as a liability. That is likely to lead to a protracted period of both higher commodity prices and price volatility until we get a situation of much more strategic predictability (along with the consequences) and far more thorough execution of that strategy.

To me that makes ConocoPhillips at best a hold. This company, at least initially, is in no position to benefit from higher oil prices at first when its assets are suffering damage from the situation that likely exceeds the benefits. How much more damage cannot be currently determined. Mr. Market always worries about losses more so than profits or good news.

But when I cannot tell what is going on, I cannot value the company. For many, that may make this company a sell. That would especially be true if the current situation drags on for an indeterminate period of time.

Risks

Once we get to a point where assets cannot be damaged anymore, then there is a possibility that this company could benefit from commodity prices that will remain high while repairs are underway. There could well be insurance coverage for at least part of the damage.

But right now, both the industry and investors need a whole lot less uncertainty.

There also needs to be an effort by the administration to understand this industry so that it can provide for some very basic industry needs. Currently the industry appears to be "hunkering down" for an extended period of uncertainty. But that could show itself as a lack of production growth at a time when that production growth may be sorely needed.

To summarize, the risk of loss at this point in time is very real due to the uncertainty that lies ahead. This is not only true about the Iranian situation. But it also extends to the changeability and inconsistency of the tariffs policy. This is on top of the basic industry feelings that the industry is not well understood and therefore not supported by what is supposedly a friendly administration.

An increase in certainty on a number of presidential administration-controlled "fronts" is sorely needed.

data.title: Janus Henderson Triton Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Fund returned 4.75% and the Russell 2500™ Growth Index returned 0.33%.

Contributors/detractors

Stock selection in the healthcare sector contributed to relative performance. Stock selection in the real estate sector detracted.

Outlook

We see a favorable environment for small-cap stocks, given their attractive relative valuations and a more accommodative backdrop for mergers and acquisitions.

Investment environment

• Stocks rose in the fourth quarter on positive corporate earnings news and hopes for Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts. The Fed followed up a September rate cut with two 25 basis-point (bp) rate cuts, in October and December, respectively.

• Economic growth remains resilient despite policy headwinds and slower-than-expected job growth. Inflation also has been relatively subdued.

• The quarter saw some recalibration of the exuberance around artificial intelligence (AI) that characterized the market in 2025. As a result, we saw a broadening of the market and renewed focus on fundamentals, which benefited our fundamentals-driven investment approach.

Portfolio review

We were pleased to see many of our investments rewarded during the period, especially in the healthcare sector. Praxis Precision Medicines, a top performer, is a clinical-stage biopharmaceuticals company that uses genetic information to develop treatments for central-nervous-system conditions. The company has gained more attention from investors after reporting positive late-stage trial results for several products. These included Vormatrigine, an investigational anti-seizure medication that has shown effectiveness in addressing treatment-resistant epilepsy. Even with recent outperformance, the stock trades at a discount to its peers.

Given that Praxis has multiple products in late-stage trials, we see several potential catalysts that could drive further investor interest in the company.

Globus Medical was another contributor. The medical device maker was a relative detractor earlier in the year, when it suffered a period of slower organic growth while integrating several acquisitions. We held onto the stock because of our confidence in the company's management team and track record of successful acquisitions. Our confidence paid off: The stock rebounded strongly in the fourth quarter as margin expansion and robust sales of spine products led to better-than-expected earnings growth and increased guidance. Additionally, we remain constructive on the potential synergies from Globus's recent purchase of Nevro Corp., another medical device company that focuses on spine health.

Teledyne Technologies was a relative detractor. Teledyne provides digital imaging products, as well as electronic subsystems and instrumentation products for industrial, aerospace, and defense applications. The stock was a strong performer through most of the year but declined in the fourth quarter despite Teledyne's solid third-quarter results. Investors were disappointed that the company did not raise its full-year guidance even higher after these results, but we believe the management team has been relatively conservative in forecasting fourth-quarter growth. We remain constructive on the company's earnings growth outlook given its stable recurring revenue growth and sizable orders backlog. Teledyne has also been disciplined in capital allocation, completing prudent acquisitions while returning money to shareholders through stock buybacks.

Education technology company Stride (LRN) was a relative detractor. Stride provides a remote learning platform for both K-12 education and professional training. The stock has been a strong performer in recent quarters, supported by the company's rapid enrollment growth. Recently, however, Stride encountered technological issues with a platform upgrade that resulted in login issues, user dissatisfaction, and modest student churn, which was more than offset by overall enrollment growth. While the company has worked to address these technological issues, it scaled back its marketing efforts and growth targets. This led to reduced guidance and a sell-off in the stock. We saw this market response as excessive, since Stride still expects revenues to grow at a double-digit pace in 2026. We also believe the company can return to its previous growth trajectory once it addresses these technological issues.

Manager outlook

We see many reasons for optimism in 2026, given the resilience of consumer spending and broader economic growth. Lower interest rates should create a supportive funding environment for smaller companies, while also benefiting valuations of longer-duration growth businesses. That said, we recognize that the extent of Fed easing will depend on continued moderation in inflation. Concerns about government borrowing and the independence of the Fed could also put upward pressure on longer-term yields, which could act as a headwind for market performance.

We continue to see a favorable environment for small-cap stocks, given their attractive relative valuations and a more accommodative backdrop for mergers and acquisitions. We also remain excited about investment opportunities tied to powerful secular growth trends. These include healthcare innovation, electrification, nearshoring, and AI, which we see as powerful, multi-year investment themes with the potential to transform the economy. In seeking to capitalize on these trends we continue to adhere to our investment philosophy – seeking companies with strong competitive positions, proven management teams, and the prospect of high returns on capital coupled with strong free cash flow. We also remain committed to investing in companies with reasonable valuations, leading us to look beyond some high-profile stocks – many of which are trading at elevated valuations – to compelling ideas that may be underappreciated by other investors. These include power companies that stand to benefit from surging electricity demand tied to AI adoption and tight U.S. power capacity. These businesses often trade at significantly lower price-to-earnings ratios relative to pure-play AI companies, and we believe they may offer an attractive avenue for capitalizing on the AI buildout.

data.title: Fidelity Small Cap Index Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Performance Review

The fund finished the fourth quarter slightly ahead of the 2.19% advance of the Russell 2000® Index. Securities lending income, corporate action activity and security misweights due to certain benchmark securities that the Fund is restricted from buying all aided results this period. Efficient trading and implementation strategies also helped limit fund transaction costs while replicating the exposures and characteristics of the index.

U.S. small-cap stocks posted a gain in the fourth quarter, according to the Russell index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance has been supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence and the Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reductions since December 2024. Amid this favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed the year just shy of its all-time high.

Stocks entered October on an uptrend, but with the federal government in a shutdown that would last until November 12. On October 29, the central bank lowered its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25 percentage points at its second consecutive meeting. The index gained 1.81% for the month, with the Fed pushing back against market expectations for a further rate cut in 2025. Momentum faded a bit in November (+0.96%), despite the continued shutdown and a brief mid-month sell-off that mostly reflected concern about an AI bubble. Meanwhile, the Fed cut rates by another quarter point on December 10. The index returned -0.58% for the month, a 12.81% gain for the year and a 42.37% increase since the low on April 8.

In the fourth quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented health care sector (+19%), which particularly shined in November. Materials (+5%) also delivered a solid gain. The growth-oriented communication services sector advanced 4%, led by telecommunication services stocks (-9%). All other groups lagged the index. Information technology, which represented 15% of the index in Q4, returned -5%. Consumer staples (-5%) and consumer discretionary (-4%) also delivered weak results. The utilities (-2%) and industrials (-2%) sectors each lost ground, and energy (+1%) was held back by a sharp decline in the price of oil. Meanwhile, real estate (+2%) came in just under the index. Financials (+2%) also delivered a modest gain.

Regardless of the market environment, we continue to apply a disciplined investment process across all our strategies, relying on highly skilled professionals and robust investment infrastructure. Investment performance is the foundation of our value proposition for shareholders. This is true of our comprehensive suite of low-cost index funds. We expect our index funds to deliver low tracking difference, which is the difference in a fund's performance to that of its stated benchmark. We also seek to minimize tracking error, which measures the volatility of these return differences over a period of time. Whether it's through solid trading techniques for funds that replicate an index or our optimization techniques, when necessary, we are focused on delivering returns in line with benchmark performance. CHARACTERISTICS

Earnings-Per-Share Growth Trailing measures the growth in reported earnings per share over trailing one- and five-year periods.

Earnings-Per-Share Growth (IBES 1-Year Forecast) measures the growth in reported earnings per share as estimated by Wall Street analysts.

Median Market Cap identifies the median market capitalization of the portfolio or benchmark as determined by the underlying security market caps.

Price-to-Book (P/B) Ratio is the ratio of a company's current share price to reported accumulated profits and capital.

Price/Cash Flow is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months cash flow per share.

Price-to-Earnings ((P/E)) Ratio (IBES 1-Year Forecast) is the ratio of a company's current share price to Wall Street analysts' estimates of earnings.

Price-to-Earnings ((P/E)) Ratio Trailing is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months earnings per share.

Return on Equity (ROE) 5-Year Trailing is the ratio of a company's last five years historical profitability to its shareholders' equity. Preferred stock is included as part of each company's net worth.

Sales-Per-Share Growth measures the growth in reported sales over the specified past time period.

Weighted Average Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the average equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

Weighted Median Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the median equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

3-YEAR RISK/RETURN Statistics

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (less) than 1 is more (less) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.
